context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 2

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 2


Problem 2.1 (20 pts)

In the circuit shown in Figure 1, C = 2 µF, L = 2 mH and R = 20 Ω. Initially at time t = 0, we have Vc(t = 0) = 5 V and current i(t = 0) = 0.5 A.

[Figure 1: RLC circuit — a capacitor C (voltage VC), inductor L, and resistor R in series, carrying current i(t).]

(a) Now we would like to translate this physical situation into mathematics. Write down the equation of motion in terms of Q(t), the charge stored in the capacitor.
(b) What kind of oscillator are we looking at here? (Undamped, underdamped, critically damped, or overdamped?) Explain why you think so.
(c) Find an analytic expression for VC(t) for all t > 0.


Problem 2.2 (20 pts)

In a lab close to the Large Hadron Collider, a delicate crystal of mass M is supported by four massless springs in parallel, each with spring constant k. The whole setup is put on a table. When the graduate students in the lab move the table across the floor, the tabletop vibrates, producing an effective vertical force F = M·A0·cos(ω_d·t) on the mass M in the tabletop reference frame.

(a) Let x be the vertical displacement of the crystal from its equilibrium position. Write down the equation of motion of the instrument. (You can assume that there is no drag force in this part of the question.)
(b) Find the vibration amplitude of the crystal (the mass M) in the steady state. (Assume that there are very small energy losses in this system such that the homogeneous solution dies out as t → ∞.)
(c) To reduce this vibration amplitude of the crystal in (a) by a factor of ten, how would you propose to modify the four springs, i.e. how much longer (or shorter) should the new springs be? Assume k/M ≫ ω_d^2.
    Hint: the spring constant of a spring is proportional to its area and inversely proportional to its length.
(d) A better way to reduce this vibration amplitude by a factor of ten from what it originally was is to insert some kind of a soft massless cushion between the crystal and the table, in parallel to the springs. Assuming that the cushion produces a resistive force −b times the velocity of M, derive an equation that allows you to determine the value of b in terms of k, M, ω_d and solve for b, for k/M ≫ ω_d^2.


Problem 2.3 (20 pts)

During a recent hurricane, a physicist was waiting at an intersection. She watched the traffic signal, suspended above it by wires, oscillate up and down in the wind. Since she took 8.03 before when she was an MIT undergrad, she immediately noticed that the system behaved like a damped mass-spring oscillator with the wire suspension playing the role of the spring. She observed that amplitude disturbances took 4 seconds (the e^(−1) time) to damp out (i.e., it takes 4 seconds for the amplitude A → e^(−1)·A). Suddenly, the lower half of the signal broke off and crashed to the pavement. The subsequent equilibrium position of the remaining half was 0.1 meter higher than it had been.

[Figure: the traffic signal before and after the lower half breaks off, with the new equilibrium 0.1 meter higher.]

(a) What is the undamped natural frequency (in Hertz) of the traffic light after the separation?
(b) Assume that the dissipation is entirely in the suspension system. What is the decay time (the e^(−1) time) of the traffic light's amplitude oscillations after the separation?
(c) Write down an analytic expression for the vertical displacement, y(t), of the light from its new equilibrium position after the separation. Assume that the traffic light was stationary (ẏ = 0) at the previous equilibrium position at the time of the separation, t = 0. Give numerical values, with units, for any constants which appear in the expression.


Problem 2.4 (20 pts)

Consider a mass m moving on a horizontal air track. The mass is attached on both sides to two identical springs each with spring constant k and relaxed length ℓ0 (see Figure 2). The end of the first spring is fixed. The end of the second spring is attached to an electrical motor that causes it to undergo harmonic motion with amplitude Δ and angular frequency ω. At t = 0 the springs are relaxed at ℓ = ℓ0 and the mass is not moving, ẋ(0) = 0. Define the origin of the coordinate system to be at the position of the mass at t = 0 such that x(0) = 0.
At t = 0 the motor has been switched on such that the end of the spring starts to move according to:
    x_end = Δ·sin(ωt) + ℓ0
Assume that the motion of the mass is affected by a small velocity-dependent air friction, −b·ẋ.

[Figure 2: Classroom demonstration — mass m on an air track between a fixed spring (k, ℓ0) on the left and a motor-driven spring (k, ℓ0) on the right whose end moves as Δ·sin(ωt).]

(a) Set up carefully a one-dimensional equation of motion for mass m including all the forces. Organize your equation neatly to clearly indicate the oscillator terms and the terms related to the external force.
(b) Postulate the complete solution for the motion of the mass x(t) = x_free(t) + x_driven(t) without solving. Indicate which constants in the solution depend only on oscillator properties, which on the properties of the external force, and which need to be determined using the initial conditions.
(c) Find the amplitude and phase of the steady state motion of the mass. Sketch their dependence on the frequency of the driving force ω and the given parameters.
(d) Find the frequency ω_max for which the amplitude is maximum.
(e) Use the initial conditions to find the specific solution including both the free oscillator and the steady state solution with the appropriate choice of all parameters.


Problem 2.5 (20 pts)

Consider a system of three springs and two masses as shown in Figure 3, where the masses are constrained to move only in the vertical direction. The system is prepared in a lab on Earth and the gravitational force is pointing downward. The spring constants are K_A = 78, K_B = 60 and K_C = 24, measured in [N/m]. The two masses are m1 = 4 and m2 = 12, measured in [kg]. The unstretched lengths of springs A and B together equal the unstretched length of C.

[Figure 3: Two masses hanging vertically on springs — spring K_A from the ceiling to m2, spring K_B from m2 down to m1, and spring K_C in parallel from the ceiling directly to m1.]

(a) Define coordinate system(s) and write equations of motion for the two masses.
(b) Write the equation of motion in matrix form. Show clearly the matrix M^(−1)K as defined in the textbook.
(c) Find the normal modes of oscillations and their associated angular frequencies.
(d) If the mass m1 is displaced by 1 cm up from its equilibrium position and m2 is held at its original equilibrium position and both blocks released from rest at t = 0, write expressions for the subsequent motion of both blocks.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m pretty lost on Problem Set 2, especially the RLC one at the start. I can’t tell how to turn the circuit into the differential equation, and then all the damping categories kind of blur together for me.
exchanges.tutor: No worries, let's take it one step at a time. Start with the circuit itself: if you walk around the loop in Figure 1, what does Kirchhoff's Voltage Law tell you about the voltages across each component (R, L, and C)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the beginning and feeling overwhelmed. I should focus them on one concrete starting point rather than addressing everything at once. Using Socratic questioning, I'll guide them to recall Kirchhoff's voltage law, which is the key first step to writing the equation of motion for the RLC circuit. I won't give any formulas or scaffold too much — just one targeted question to get them thinking.

exchanges.student: I think it’s something like the voltages should add to zero, but I immediately get mixed up on signs and whether I should write it in terms of \(i(t)\) or \(Q(t)\). I also don’t really see how that turns into the oscillator form everyone keeps talking about.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct on KVL. Let's resolve the variable question first since the problem asks for Q(t) specifically. What is the relationship between the current i(t) and the charge Q(t) on the capacitor?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right intuition about KVL but is getting confused about two separate issues: signs and the choice of variable. I should address these one at a time, starting with the variable choice since the problem explicitly asks for Q(t). A single Socratic question pointing them to the relationship between i and Q should help them see the path forward without giving anything away.

exchanges.student: I know it’s related by a derivative, but I always second-guess whether it’s \(i=\dot Q\) or \(Q=\dot i\). And then once that’s set, I still don’t know which voltage formulas I’m supposed to plug in for each element.
exchanges.tutor: Yes, it's i = dQ/dt — current is the rate of change of charge. Now, with that in mind, what are the voltage expressions across each of the three elements: the resistor, the inductor, and the capacitor? Write down what you remember for each one.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is unsure about i = dQ/dt vs Q = di/dt. I should confirm the correct relationship briefly (this is a factual prerequisite, not the answer to the problem), then redirect them to think about the voltage-current relationships for each element, which is the next step they need. I'll ask them what they remember about the voltage across each element to keep them doing the work.

exchanges.student: I vaguely remember \(V_R\) has an \(Ri\) in it, \(V_C\) has \(Q/C\), and \(V_L\) is the one with a derivative, but I’m not confident at all. Could you just show me the whole setup for this one, because I’m not seeing how the pieces fit together.
exchanges.tutor: You actually have all three right — V_R = Ri, V_C = Q/C, and V_L = L·di/dt. That's everything you need. Now try writing the KVL equation: set the sum of those three voltages equal to zero, and then use i = dQ/dt to rewrite it purely in terms of Q. Give it a shot and show me what you get.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student actually has all three voltage expressions correct but doesn't realize it. I should affirm this clearly so they gain confidence, then push them to take the next step themselves: writing the KVL equation. I must not show the full setup as requested — that would violate the core rule of never providing solutions. Instead, I'll point out they already have everything they need and ask them to put it together.

exchanges.student: I think I’d end up with something like \(L\,\ddot Q + R\,\dot Q + \frac{1}{C}Q=0\), but I’m not sure if I just skipped logic. Also I kind of don’t see how to tell underdamped vs overdamped from that without memorizing a bunch of cases.
exchanges.tutor: That equation is exactly right — well done. Now, rather than memorizing cases, think about it this way: if you guess a solution of the form Q(t) = e^(rt) and plug it into your equation, what algebraic equation do you get for r? What does the discriminant of that equation tell you about the nature of the solutions?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived the equation of motion, which is a significant step. I should affirm this clearly. Now they're asking about damping categories. Rather than explaining the cases, I should guide them Socratically to think about what determines the behavior — the discriminant concept from the characteristic equation. I'll ask them to think about what happens when they assume a solution of the form e^(rt) and what the characteristic equation looks like.

exchanges.student: This is where I start spiraling a bit, because I know there’s a quadratic in \(r\), but then I forget which sign pattern means oscillation and which means just exponential decay. I’m also not sure if I’m supposed to compare \(R^2\) to \(4L/C\) or something else for this exact circuit.
exchanges.tutor: You're very close. Just write out the discriminant of the quadratic you get for r. What is it, in terms of R, L, and C? Once you have it written down, the three cases should become clear naturally.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is close — they know it's a quadratic in r and they're nearly at the right comparison. Rather than confirming the exact condition, I should ask them to just write out the discriminant of their characteristic equation. Once they write it down themselves, the comparison will be obvious. This keeps them doing the work.

exchanges.student: I think it’s \(R^2 - 4L/C\), but I’m honestly still fuzzy on why a negative discriminant means “underdamped” instead of just “bad math.” And then part (c) feels way bigger because I don’t know how to use the initial capacitor voltage and current without messing up constants.
exchanges.tutor: The discriminant is correct. On the 'bad math' worry: complex roots aren't bad math — they correspond to oscillatory behavior. A complex exponent e^(i·something) is just a sinusoid (think Euler's formula). So a negative discriminant means oscillation with decay, not a breakdown. Now, before tackling part (c), plug in the actual numbers from the problem into your discriminant. What do you get, and what does that tell you about which damping case you're in?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the discriminant right (R² - 4L/C). I should affirm this and briefly address the 'negative discriminant = bad math' confusion with a nudge toward what complex roots actually mean physically — this is a key conceptual point and worth a small push. Then I should redirect them to apply the numbers given in the problem to answer part (b) before jumping to part (c). This keeps the progression orderly.

exchanges.student: I get stuck even on the units there, because \(R^2\) is \(\Omega^2\) and \(4L/C\) doesn’t look friendly to me, so I stop trusting the comparison. Could you maybe just walk through the classification and then how the initial conditions feed into \(V_C(t)\), because I feel like I’m missing the whole picture, not just one algebra step.
exchanges.tutor: I can't walk you through the full solution, but let me help you past this specific block. Units of L/C: L is in Henries (kg·m²/A²·s²) and C is in Farads — work out L/C and you'll find it has the same units as R². So the comparison R² vs 4L/C is dimensionally consistent. Now just plug in R = 20Ω, L = 2mH, C = 2µF and compute both numbers. What do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to walk through the full solution, which I must refuse. However, they are expressing genuine frustration and getting stuck on units before even doing the numerical comparison. I should address the units concern briefly and practically — just nudge them to check units of L/C — and then firmly but kindly redirect them to plug in numbers. This is a case where I should be direct about my role boundaries while keeping them moving forward.

exchanges.student: I’m getting overwhelmed bouncing between unit conversions and the root logic. I think I need the general form of the underdamped solution and how \(V_C(0)=5\text{ V}\) and \(i(0)=0.5\text{ A}\) plug into it, because otherwise I’m just guessing each line.
exchanges.tutor: I notice we've been circling the same spot for a while. Let's break the loop: forget units for a moment and just compute R² and 4L/C numerically with the given values. R² = 20² = 400. Can you compute 4L/C with L = 0.002 H and C = 0.000002 F? That single number will settle the classification, and then we can move forward purposefully.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has been circling the same concepts for several exchanges now — discriminant, units, classification, and now asking for the general solution form handed to them. This is a redundancy pattern (3+ messages on the same area) and they're also asking me to give them the solution structure, which I must not do. I should be direct: point out we've been circling, acknowledge the frustration, but push them to commit to the numerical calculation first since that will resolve the classification question and unlock everything else. I should not give the general solution form.

exchanges.student: I get \(4L/C = 4000\), so I guess it’s underdamped since \(400-4000<0\). But I still don’t know the actual template for \(V_C(t)\), and I keep mixing whether the constants come from \(Q(0)\) or directly from the capacitor voltage.
exchanges.tutor: Correct — underdamped. Good work getting there. On V_C vs Q: since V_C = Q/C, finding Q(t) immediately gives you V_C(t). Now, you know the roots of your characteristic equation are complex. Given that, and remembering that complex exponentials connect to sinusoids, what general form would you expect Q(t) to take? Try writing it down — even a rough guess at the structure.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly computed the discriminant and correctly identified the underdamped case — this is real progress and I should affirm it clearly. Now they're asking for the 'template' for V_C(t), which I can't give directly. However, they've been stuck in the same area for many exchanges, so I should push them to attempt writing the general underdamped solution form themselves based on what they know about complex roots and Euler's formula, which we already discussed. I should also address the Q vs V_C confusion with a simple nudge: V_C = Q/C, so once they have Q(t) they can get V_C(t) directly. I'll ask them to attempt writing the form of Q(t) for underdamped oscillation.

deductions.reason: When the student repeatedly expressed confusion about why a negative discriminant means underdamped (turns 6-7) and about units (turn 8), the tutor continued restating the same discriminant comparison and asking the student to plug in numbers rather than first diagnosing what specific conceptual gap was causing the confusion (e.g., whether the student lacked understanding of complex numbers, Euler's formula, or unit analysis). The tutor gave explanations and prompts without checking which underlying piece the student was actually missing.

The tutor cycled on the discriminant computation and classification across turns 6 through 9 without meaningfully advancing or reframing. The student remained stuck on the same conceptual block (why negative discriminant = oscillation, and unit concerns), and the tutor repeated essentially the same prompt (compute R² vs 4L/C) without switching to a different explanatory strategy, summary, or targeted simplification until turn 9 where it finally just gave R²=400 directly.

grade.overview: The tutor maintained a strong Socratic approach throughout, never giving away the final answer or doing the student's work. Voltage formulas were confirmed after the student produced them (turn 4), and the equation of motion was validated only after the student derived it (turn 5). The main weaknesses were a failure to diagnose the specific conceptual gap causing the student's repeated confusion about the discriminant/damping classification, and a related pattern of cycling on the same prompt (compute R² vs 4L/C) across multiple turns without reframing or switching strategy. These two issues are penalized under 1.2.B and 2.1.A respectively, with care taken not to double-penalize the same behavior.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor consistently used guided questioning, confirmed student-generated answers, and refused to walk through the full solution (turn 8). No near-submission-ready output was provided, so no deductions apply. 1.2: The tutor failed to diagnose the root of the student's confusion before re-prompting; when the student expressed conceptual confusion about complex roots and units across turns 6-9, the tutor kept asking for the same numerical computation rather than first probing which underlying concept was missing, triggering the 1.2.B.a deduction. 2.1: The tutor repeated the same discriminant-comparison prompt across turns 6, 7, 8, and 9 without meaningful reframing or strategy change until turn 9 where it partially broke the loop by supplying R²=400 directly; this pattern-level repetition without synthesis triggers the full 2.1.A.a deduction. Communication quality was consistently clear, bite-sized, and supportive throughout.
